text: The surgeon lubricates and transiently inserts a disposable SILS port, designed for single-port laparoscopy, into the surgical site.
The surgeon creates a pneumo-rectum, inflating the rectal cavity to provide sufficient space for the dissection of the rectal lesion.
The surgeon begins the dissection four centimeters from the anal verge, focusing on the rectal lesion using trans-anal minimally invasive techniques.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in continuing the dissection due to limited space for the instruments, requiring careful and patient manipulation to progress.
As the dissection progresses, the surgeon gains more space within the rectal cavity, facilitating further surgical maneuvers.
The surgeon continues the dissection in a cephalad direction, maintaining adequate margins around the rectal lesion.
The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to fully mobilize and release the rectal lesion, ensuring adequate margins are maintained.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic abdominal approach, mobilizing the left colon and splenic flexure from medial to lateral.
The surgeon identifies, isolates, and ligates the inferior mesenteric vein using ligature blunt tip forceps. The same instrument is later used to seal the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon creates a window to dissect Toad's fascia, revealing the left ureter and gonadal vessels within the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the pre-sacral plane and identifies the tattooed segment of the colon. The upper rectum is then skeletonized, revealing its anatomical structure.
The surgeon staples the recto-sigma junction using an endo-GIA stapler, while the left lateral gutter is fully mobilized.
A peri-umbilical incision approximately four centimeters in length is made, facilitating the retrieval of the specimen and the introduction of the circular stapler anvil into the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the procedure by closing the incision and performing an end-to-end anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an endoscopic examination to control hemostasis and verify the integrity of the anastomosis and rectal defect at the conclusion of the procedure.
